significance: Ming granted it uniquely favourable tribute terms, making a tiny kingdom the licensed conduit that laundered wakō smuggling into lawful China trade.
The sole Portuguese enclave on the China coast — the Canton–Nagasaki silk-for-silver relay and Europe's back-door to China.
Korea's one licensed foreign post — the walled Tsushima-Japanese factory that mirrored Dejima on the opposite shore.
A cosmopolitan royal-monopoly capital-port with Dutch, French, Persian, Japanese and Chinese quarters, until its 1767 sack.
A monsoon port with permanent Japanese and Chinese quarters — the Nguyễn state's window for the Japan–Vietnam silk trade.
Terminus of the Manila galleon — one ship a year swapping American silver for Chinese silk, welding Acapulco to Asia.
A free-trade sultanate that smuggled monopoly spices past the VOC until conquered — the loophole in the Dutch cartel.
The world's only source of nutmeg and mace — a few tiny islands the VOC depopulated in 1621 to hold the monopoly.
A Malay port-queendom that absorbed trade redirected after Melaka's 1511 fall and hosted an early Portuguese factory.
The Coromandel outlet whose kalamkari cottons clothed Southeast Asia and Persia — textiles, not tonnage.
The Malabar pepper port where Vasco da Gama landed in 1498 — spice emporium of an indigenous Hindu polity.
The fortified Dutch port through which the VOC's monopoly on the world's finest cinnamon flowed.
Source of the cowries that served as money across Bengal and West Africa — a currency mine, not a bulk port.
The Bab-el-Mandeb toll-port and world coffee market that named the bean; every Red Sea ship was forced to call.
Seat of an Arab sea-empire that expelled the Portuguese and ran a network to Zanzibar — an indigenous naval power's base.
The Safavid-held foothold at the Strait of Hormuz where Dutch, English and Persian interests collided.
A VOC pearl-fishery fort on a Gulf island, seized back by the corsair Mir Muhanna — the Dutch Gulf endgame.
The fortress that changed hands between Portugal and Oman — the flashpoint of the Swahili coast.
The principal Indian-Ocean pirate base — significant purely for its position astride the Indies route (Kidd, Tew, Every).
The oldest European fort in sub-Saharan Africa — first a gold castle, later a slaving depot.
The French river-mouth entrepôt for gum arabic — the textile fixative Europe's dyers could not do without.
An African-run polity whose Efik merchants brokered the Biafran slave (then palm-oil) trade on their own terms.
Russia's sole seaport before St Petersburg — an ice-bound White Sea window worked by the English Muscovy Company.
A seasonal whaling town rendering blubber at 79°N — a summer-only port that existed for oil alone.
Home port of the pocket duchy that improbably colonised Tobago and an island in the Gambia.
Base of the Brandenburg African Company and Fort Groß Friedrichsburg — Prussia's brief Atlantic-slaving bid.
A small, fiercely independent merchant republic whose neutral flag carried Ottoman–Christian trade past Venice.
The Ottoman terminus of the Levant trade, where 'Levant Company' factories gathered Persian silk and Anatolian goods.
The Black Sea's great slave market, funnelling captives from the Tatar raids into the Ottoman world.
A fever-ridden village that hosted the galleon fair — for a few weeks a year, Potosí's silver crossed the isthmus here.
'The Golden Rock' — a free port so busy smuggling arms (including to the American rebels) that Britain sacked it in 1781.
Capital of Russian America, existing to feed the Canton fur market — a monopoly company's Pacific outpost.
France's fortified cod-and-transshipment base — its cod exports were worth more than the fur trade.
